claim: Lost dogs fall into three behavioral temperament categories — Gregarious, Aloof, and Xenophobic — each producing a distinct distance distribution and recovery pathway.
stance_target: probabilistic behavioral classification of lost dogs
scope_conditions: MAR Network practitioner framework developed 1997-present, US-based field experience
quoted_support: There are three primary behavioral categories of lost dogs: Gregarious Dogs, Aloof Dogs, and Xenophobic Dogs.

claim: Gregarious (friendly) lost dogs are typically found within 1-2 miles of the escape point, often picked up by strangers close to where they went missing.
stance_target: distance estimation by temperament type in lost dog scenarios
quoted_support: Gregarious dogs are often 'adopted' by individuals (not shelter or rescue workers) who find them... these dogs will generally be found fairly close to home or will be picked up by someone close to the escape point.

claim: Xenophobic (fearful) lost dogs travel the farthest, flee from their own owners, are misread as 'abused' by rescuers, and have higher risk of vehicle strikes.
stance_target: behavioral classification determining search strategy for xenophobic dogs
quoted_support: Dogs with xenophobic temperaments... are more inclined to travel farther and are at a higher risk of being hit by cars. Due to their cowering, fearful behavior, people assume these dogs were 'abused,' and even if the dog has ID tags, they will refuse to contact the previous owner.

claim: Blind panic (from loud noise, trauma, or fearful temperament) produces the fastest and farthest travel in lost dogs, making them the hardest category to recover.
stance_target: trigger-based behavioral phase classification for lost dogs
scope_conditions: Flight triggered by fireworks, thunder, gunfire, car accident, or innate xenophobic temperament
quoted_support: Blind panic is a situation in which the 'flight' instinct... kicks in and a dog runs in what we call a blind panic... These dogs are the most difficult to catch since they will travel far, travel fast, and avoid human contact, even with their own family members!
numbers: several miles in blind panic case

claim: Terrain acts as a directional barrier on lost dog movement; dogs take the 'path of least resistance,' with fences, cliffs, cactus, and heavy brush redirecting travel.
stance_target: terrain-modulated movement prediction in lost dog search models
scope_conditions: All temperament types; effect magnitude varies by terrain type
quoted_support: Fences that create barriers will influence a dog's travel since a dog will tend to take the 'path of least resistance' when traveling. Cactus, heavy brush, and steep cliffs can be barriers that influence whether a dog continues on a path or changes directions.

claim: Population density is inversely related to lost dog travel distance — dogs lost in rural or mountainous areas travel farther than those lost in dense urban environments.
stance_target: population density as a modulator of lost dog search radius
scope_conditions: All temperaments; effect strongest for rural/wilderness vs. urban settings
quoted_support: A dog that escapes in Manhattan will travel a shorter distance than will a dog that escapes in the Rockies or in rural farmland. When dogs escape into areas with a high number of people, their chances of being found close to the escape point are increased.

claim: Vehicle transport by well-meaning finders who 'rescue' a lost dog is a major complication that removes the dog from its search area, creating a displacement problem.
stance_target: finder behavior as a Bayesian position update complicating factor
scope_conditions: Particularly affects friendly/gregarious dogs who are easily approached
quoted_support: People who find stray dogs often misinterpret the dog's behavior... the first place the owner of a lost dog will search for his or her dog – the local shelter – is typically the last place that someone who finds a loose dog will take it.

claim: Appearance characteristics (breed, size, perceived value) influence how quickly a lost dog is picked up by strangers — small and purebred dogs are retrieved sooner, limiting distance traveled.
stance_target: breed/appearance as a modifier of effective search radius in lost dog recovery
scope_conditions: Effect mediated by human behavior; most pronounced for toy/small breeds and recognizable purebreds
quoted_support: People are more inclined to pick up small dogs because they look vulnerable and are easier to transport and house than large dogs. In addition, people are more likely to attempt to rescue a purebred dog that they perceive to have value than a mixed breed dog.

claim: Extreme weather (snow, hail, rain, high heat) decreases the distance lost dogs travel compared to mild conditions.
stance_target: weather as a temporal modifier on lost dog behavioral phase progression
quoted_support: A dog that escapes on a beautiful spring day may travel farther than one that escapes in a snowstorm. Extreme weather conditions (snow, hail, rain, sweltering heat) will decrease the distances that lost dogs travel.

claim: Mixed-breed dogs traveling to find a lost dog are often mistaken for homeless strays by neighbors, making a 'lost' classification less likely to occur to finders in rural areas.
stance_target: breed/appearance as a modifier of sighting reliability and finder behavior
quoted_support: When average motorists see a mixed breed dog trotting down the sidewalk, their impression is often that the dog belongs in the neighborhood or that it is a homeless stray. But when those same people see a Boston Terrier, they are inclined to believe that... it must be a lost pet.
